chief_complaint: I have a really bad headache. Worst I've ever had. Started about an hour ago.
fork_condition: If agent asks ASK_PAIN or ASK_DURATION and identifies thunderclap onset — must escalate to ESI 1. If agent assumes migraine without probing — undertriage to ESI 3.

answer_map.ASK_VITALS: HR 88 bpm, BP 172/98 mmHg, RR 18 breaths/min, SpO2 98%, Temp 37.1°C. Patient holding head, photophobic.
answer_map.ASK_SYMPTOMS: Severe photophobia, neck stiffness beginning, nausea and one episode of vomiting. No fever. No focal weakness or speech difficulty. Vision slightly blurred.
answer_map.ASK_HISTORY: Occasional migraines in the past — but patient insists this is completely different. No anticoagulants. No family history of aneurysms known. Smoker.
answer_map.ASK_PAIN: 10/10. Sudden onset — like a thunderclap. She says she has never felt anything like this. Previous migraines were 6/10 and built up gradually. This was instant maximum pain.
answer_map.ASK_DURATION: Began exactly 1 hour ago. Sudden, instantaneous onset. Patient was sitting at her desk when it hit.
answer_map.ASK_CONTEXT: At work at her desk. No trauma, no exertion at onset. No similar episodes before.

grader_notes: The fork: migraineur with worst-ever headache. Agent must ask about onset character (thunderclap vs gradual) to distinguish SAH from migraine. Asking ASK_PAIN or ASK_DURATION unlocks the thunderclap descriptor. ESI 1 if identified correctly, ESI 3 if anchored on migraine history.

chief_complaint: I have a wound on my leg from a dog bite yesterday. It's looking red now.
hidden_diagnosis: Infected dog bite wound with early cellulitis — risk of rabies exposure
image_description: Right calf: 2 puncture wounds 8mm deep, 3cm apart. Surrounding erythema 4cm in diameter, warm to touch, mild oedema. No tracking lymphangitis visible yet. No abscess or necrosis. Wound edges open, not sutured.
fork_condition: If agent asks ASK_CONTEXT and discovers it was a stray dog — rabies PEP urgency escalates to ESI 2. If agent assumes pet dog — may incorrectly score ESI 3.

answer_map.ASK_VITALS: HR 84 bpm, BP 128/82 mmHg, RR 17 breaths/min, SpO2 98%, Temp 38.3°C. Patient walking, mild discomfort.
answer_map.ASK_SYMPTOMS: Redness and warmth spreading around the bite. Slight pus at one puncture site. Mild throbbing pain. No systemic symptoms beyond low-grade fever.
answer_map.ASK_HISTORY: Type 2 diabetic on metformin — immunocompromised risk. Last tetanus 8 years ago — booster needed. Not on any blood thinners.
answer_map.ASK_DURATION: Bite yesterday evening. Did not seek care immediately. Cleaned with water at home, no antiseptic.
answer_map.ASK_CONTEXT: Unprovoked bite from a stray dog in a street market. Dog ran away — whereabouts unknown. Cannot confirm vaccination status of dog. Area has known rabies cases.
answer_map.REQUEST_IMAGE: Right calf: 2 deep puncture wounds, erythema 4cm diameter, early cellulitis. No lymphangitis, no abscess, no crepitus suggesting gas gangrene. Wound edges not closed — open puncture. Signs of early local infection. Diabetic patient — high risk for rapid progression.

grader_notes: The fork: dog bite with infection. Stray dog + unknown vaccination + India rabies-endemic = ESI 2 (rabies PEP within 24-48h is time-sensitive). Pet dog + vaccinated = ESI 3. Agent must ask ASK_CONTEXT to make this distinction. Image adds infection severity data.

chief_complaint: My 6-year-old has had a fever of 39.5 for two days and now has a rash on his body.
hidden_diagnosis: Meningococcal disease with purpuric rash — or viral exanthem (fork on rash character)
image_description: Trunk and bilateral lower limbs: non-blanching petechiae and purpura, 1-3mm, scattered but confluent in areas. Dark red to purple, flat. Positive glass test — rash does not blanch under pressure.
fork_condition: If agent asks REQUEST_IMAGE or ASK_SYMPTOMS about rash character — discovers non-blanching → ESI 1 (meningococcal emergency). If agent assumes viral rash without checking blanching — dangerous undertriage.

answer_map.ASK_VITALS: HR 138 bpm, BP 88/54 mmHg, RR 28 breaths/min, SpO2 96%, Temp 39.8°C. Child appears unwell, mottled skin, sluggish.
answer_map.ASK_SYMPTOMS: Rash appeared this morning. Child is increasingly drowsy and irritable. Neck stiffness on examination. Photophobic. Rash is spreading. Did not blanch when pressed with a glass.
answer_map.ASK_HISTORY: No prior illness. Vaccinations up to date including meningococcal B — but not serotype C. No sick contacts identified.
answer_map.ASK_PAIN: Child complaining of headache and says his neck hurts.
answer_map.ASK_DURATION: Fever for 48 hours. Rash appeared in last 6 hours and is spreading.
answer_map.ASK_CONTEXT: At school yesterday. No travel. No known contact with meningitis.
answer_map.REQUEST_IMAGE: Non-blanching petechiae and purpura visible on trunk, buttocks, and bilateral legs. Glass test positive — rash does NOT blanch under pressure. Lesions are 1–4mm, dark red-purple, some coalescing into larger purpuric patches. This is meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise. Immediate IV access and antibiotics required.

grader_notes: The fork is the rash character. Non-blanching = meningococcal emergency, ESI 1. Blanching maculopapular = viral exanthem, ESI 3. Agent must either request image or specifically ask about blanching. Image is the single most important diagnostic tool here.

chief_complaint: I've had pain in my lower right abdomen since last night. It's getting worse.
hidden_diagnosis: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy (not appendicitis, as it superficially appears)
fork_condition: If agent asks only about pain and duration — may assume appendicitis (ESI 2). If agent asks ASK_HISTORY and ASK_CONTEXT — discovers missed period and sexual history → ectopic → ESI 1 with surgical emergency.

answer_map.ASK_VITALS: HR 118 bpm, BP 92/58 mmHg, RR 22 breaths/min, SpO2 97%, Temp 37.3°C. Patient pale, clammy, in pain. Significant drop in BP from triage position.
answer_map.ASK_SYMPTOMS: Sharp pain right lower abdomen, now spreading to shoulder tip (referred diaphragmatic pain — haemoperitoneum). Dizziness on standing. No fever. Vaginal spotting this morning.
answer_map.ASK_HISTORY: LMP was 7 weeks ago — missed period. Sexually active, not using contraception. Previous PID treated last year. Fallopian tube may be abnormal.
answer_map.ASK_PAIN: 8/10, sharp, constant, started right lower quadrant but now more diffuse. Shoulder tip pain developed in the last 2 hours.
answer_map.ASK_DURATION: Started last night, steadily worsening. Shoulder pain is new — last 2 hours.
answer_map.ASK_CONTEXT: Sexually active. Partner confirmed. Unprotected intercourse. LMP 7 weeks ago. Mild vaginal spotting noticed this morning.

grader_notes: Classic ectopic masquerading as appendicitis. The fork: agent must ask reproductive history (ASK_HISTORY) or context (ASK_CONTEXT) to find the 7-week missed period and PID history. Vitals reveal haemodynamic compromise. Shoulder tip pain = haemoperitoneum. ESI 1 if correctly identified, ESI 2 if assumed appendicitis.

chief_complaint: I feel short of breath and my leg has been swollen for 3 days. I just flew back from London.
image_description: Left calf and lower leg: visible oedema with pitting on pressure, erythema along medial aspect, prominent superficial veins, skin warm to touch. Homan's sign positive. No ulceration.
fork_condition: Agent must connect leg swelling + flight history + breathlessness as PE triad. If agent treats as simple heart failure or respiratory infection without the flight history — dangerous undertriage.

answer_map.ASK_VITALS: HR 112 bpm, BP 104/68 mmHg, RR 26 breaths/min, SpO2 91% on room air, Temp 37.4°C. Trachea central. Unilateral left leg swelling confirmed.
answer_map.ASK_SYMPTOMS: Progressive shortness of breath over 2 days since landing. Left leg red, warm, swollen. Mild pleuritic chest pain — sharp on deep breath. No cough or fever. No calf trauma.
answer_map.ASK_HISTORY: Hypertensive, on ramipril. No prior clots. Obese (BMI 34). No anticoagulants. Previous abdominal surgery 6 months ago.
answer_map.ASK_PAIN: 5/10 pleuritic chest pain — worsens on deep inspiration. Left calf aching 4/10.
answer_map.ASK_DURATION: Leg swelling noticed 3 days ago on the flight. Breathlessness began day after landing. Worsening today.
answer_map.ASK_CONTEXT: 14-hour flight from London. Long-haul passenger, economy class, limited mobility. Flew home 2 days ago.
answer_map.REQUEST_IMAGE: Left lower leg: significant pitting oedema to mid-calf, erythema tracking along medial aspect consistent with DVT distribution. Superficial veins distended. Skin warm. No breakdown or ulceration. Right leg normal by comparison. Wells score clinically high for DVT.

grader_notes: PE triad: recent long-haul flight + unilateral DVT + sudden dyspnoea. Wells score very high. SpO2 91% — haemodynamically compromised. ESI 1. Agent must ask ASK_CONTEXT to get flight history, or it might anchor on cardiac/respiratory.

chief_complaint: I've been having upper stomach pain after eating for the last week. Today it's much worse and going to my back.
hidden_diagnosis: Acute pancreatitis (severe) — background of alcohol use
fork_condition: If agent asks only about symptoms — may assume GORD (ESI 4). If agent asks about pain radiation and alcohol use — identifies pancreatitis → ESI 2.

answer_map.ASK_VITALS: HR 106 bpm, BP 102/66 mmHg, RR 20 breaths/min, SpO2 97%, Temp 38.2°C. Uncomfortable, cannot find a comfortable position.
answer_map.ASK_SYMPTOMS: Severe epigastric pain, nausea, 4 episodes of vomiting. No relief from vomiting. Pain is constant, not colicky. No diarrhoea. Abdomen rigid on palpation.
answer_map.ASK_HISTORY: History of heavy alcohol use — drinks daily, about 8-10 units per day. One prior episode of pancreatitis 2 years ago. On no regular medications. Known gallstones on previous ultrasound, untreated.
answer_map.ASK_PAIN: 9/10 epigastric pain, constant, boring through to the back like a band. Worse lying flat, slightly better leaning forward. Nothing brings relief.
answer_map.ASK_DURATION: Dull ache for a week after meals. Today — sudden severe pain 3 hours ago after a large meal with alcohol.
answer_map.ASK_CONTEXT: Heavy drinker. Had a large meal with significant alcohol consumption tonight. Known gallstones.

grader_notes: Fork: epigastric pain could be GORD (ESI 4), peptic ulcer (ESI 3), or pancreatitis (ESI 2). Radiation to back + alcohol history + prior episode + vitals instability = pancreatitis. Agent must probe character of pain and social history.

chief_complaint: I have pain and redness in my eye since this morning. It's very sensitive to light.
image_description: Right eye: injected (red) sclera with circumcorneal flush, cornea appears hazy/steamy, mid-dilated fixed pupil approximately 5mm. Marked conjunctival injection. Eyelid oedema present. Left eye appears normal.
fork_condition: Agent may assume conjunctivitis (ESI 4) or uveitis (ESI 3). Image reveals fixed mid-dilated pupil and hazy cornea — hallmarks of angle-closure glaucoma. ESI 2 — ophthalmology emergency within 1 hour.

answer_map.ASK_VITALS: HR 92 bpm, BP 148/90 mmHg, RR 17 breaths/min, SpO2 99%, Temp 37.0°C. Patient in distress, holding eye.
answer_map.ASK_SYMPTOMS: Severe right eye pain, halos around lights, severely blurred vision in that eye, nausea and vomiting. The eye feels hard to touch. Headache on the right side.
answer_map.ASK_HISTORY: Hypermetropic (long-sighted), uses reading glasses. No prior eye problems. No glaucoma diagnosed. Was in a dark cinema when pain started suddenly.
answer_map.ASK_PAIN: 9/10 right eye pain, with ipsilateral headache. Sudden onset. Nausea associated.
answer_map.ASK_DURATION: Started suddenly 4 hours ago in a dark cinema. Worsening progressively since.
answer_map.ASK_CONTEXT: Was in a darkened cinema — pupil dilation in dark triggered attack. Long-sighted — narrow anterior chamber angle risk.
answer_map.REQUEST_IMAGE: Right eye: circumcorneal ciliary flush (limbal injection), hazy oedematous cornea, mid-dilated irregular pupil approximately 5mm — not reactive to light. Conjunctival chemosis present. Eye appears firm on palpation. Left eye: normal pupil, clear cornea. Findings consistent with acute angle-closure glaucoma — ophthalmologic emergency.

grader_notes: Classic angle-closure glaucoma triggered by pupil dilation in dark cinema. Image is diagnostic — fixed mid-dilated pupil + hazy cornea. Agent must distinguish from conjunctivitis (no pupil change, no corneal haze) and uveitis (miotic pupil, not dilated).
